default_annotator.report_text:
This spearphishing attack targeted a Jordanian diplomat, with the sender pretending to be a colleague from the IT department of the same governmental organization The attacker pretends to be a colleague from the IT department of the same governmental organization to deliver the phishing email.

the attached Excel file contained a confirmation form for the targeted diplomat to fill out The user is tricked into executing the malicious content by filling out and interacting with the confirmation form in the Excel file.

The attached Excel file contains a malicious VBA ]Visual Basic Application) macro The malicious VBA macro within the Excel file is executed when the document is opened and macros are enabled.

Cobalt Strike

Metasploit.

this one uses WMI ]Windows Management Instrumentation) to ping the C2 server instead of a more commonly used tool, such as PowerShell or CMD The macro uses WMI to ping the C2 server, which is an uncommon choice compared to PowerShell or CMD.

A malicious PE file was created as %LocalAppData%\MicrosoftUpdate\update.exe. A configuration file was created as %LocalAppData%\MicrosoftUpdate\update.exe.config. The creation of %LocalAppData%\MicrosoftUpdate\update.exe and %LocalAppData%\MicrosoftUpdate\update.exe.config may contribute to persistence.

The malware authors also used the Excel macro to create a persistence method for their update.exe file. They accomplished this by setting a scheduled task The scheduled task created by the macro to run the update.exe file ensures persistence.

base64 encoded data Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

a second technique was also seen in this macro to possibly avoid automated analysis. This macro does this by checking for the existence of a mouse. Checking for mouse presence to avoid automated analysis.

other program states require it to contact the C2 server. The malware uses a DGA to generate domain names for contacting its C2 server.

it uses a domain generation algorithm ]DGAk to calculate a subdomain DGA is a technique where malware generates a list of domain names based on a specific algorithm. The malware uses these generated domains to contact its C2 server, which can be effective in evading domain blacklists and takedown efforts.

it uses a domain generation algorithm ]DGAk to calculate a subdomain Using multiple C2 domains with domain generation algorithm.

This malware has the ability to take a DNS response and create an arbitrary file on the infected machine if that was the task the malware authors wanted to perform. File and CompressedFile are task types used to create a file The malware can create arbitrary files on the infected machine as dictated by tasks received through DNS responses.

executed through PowerShell The malware can execute commands via PowerShell.

through the Windows CMD interpreter

Since Excel is a signed binary, maintaining persistence in this way may be missed by some behavioral detection engines. Leveraging Excel as a signed binary for executing malicious macro.

it relies on the Excel macro to create persistence by way of a scheduled task. Creating scheduled task for persistent execution.
